label: What is a Computer?
intuition: A computer is a machine that follows instructions to process information.
analogy: Like a very obedient chef who can only cook exactly what the recipe says — nothing more, nothing less.
why_it_matters: Everything in AI runs on computers. Understanding what they are is the true starting point.

label: Input & Output
intuition: Computers receive information (input), process it, and send results back (output).
analogy: Like a vending machine — you put money in (input), it gives you a snack (output).
why_it_matters: AI systems are fundamentally input→output machines. GPT takes text in, gives text out.

label: Binary Numbers
intuition: Computers only understand two states: on and off — represented as 1 and 0.
analogy: Like a light switch. It's either ON (1) or OFF (0). Everything a computer does is millions of light switches flipping.
why_it_matters: Neural networks store all their knowledge as numbers — ultimately all 1s and 0s.

label: Memory & Storage
intuition: RAM is your computer's short-term memory (what it's working on now). Storage is long-term memory (files saved permanently).
analogy: RAM is your desk — you work on things there. Storage is your filing cabinet — things go there when you're done.
why_it_matters: Training large AI models requires enormous amounts of both — understanding why helps you think about AI at scale.

label: CPU & Processing
intuition: The CPU is the brain of the computer — it executes instructions one at a time, very fast.
analogy: One brilliant chef cooking dish after dish sequentially. A GPU is a thousand simpler chefs all cooking at the same time.
why_it_matters: AI training uses GPUs because matrix math is embarrassingly parallel — thousands of simple operations at once.

label: How Code Works
intuition: Code is a precise set of instructions written in a language humans and computers both understand.
analogy: Like a recipe — it says exactly what to do, in what order, using what ingredients. The computer follows it perfectly every time.
why_it_matters: Every AI system is code. You need to read, write, and understand code to build AI.

label: Python: First Steps
intuition: Python is a programming language designed to be as readable as possible — it almost reads like English.
analogy: Python is like giving instructions to a very literal-minded friend. Say exactly what you mean, and they'll do it perfectly.
why_it_matters: Python is the language of AI. Every major AI library — TensorFlow, PyTorch, scikit-learn — is Python-first.

label: Variables
intuition: A variable is a named box that stores a value. You can look inside it or put something new in it.
analogy: Like a labelled jar. Write cookies on it, put 5 cookies in. Later you can check how many or add more.
why_it_matters: All data in AI models — weights, activations, losses — are stored in variables (called tensors).

label: Data Types
intuition: Every value has a type — a number, text, or true/false. The type tells Python what operations make sense.
analogy: Like sorting mail — you treat a letter differently from a package. Python treats numbers differently from text.
why_it_matters: In ML, knowing the difference between integers and floats, and between scalars and tensors, is essential.

label: If / Else Logic
intuition: If a condition is true, do this. Otherwise, do that. Computers make decisions this way.
analogy: Like a traffic light. IF it's green THEN go. ELSE stop.
why_it_matters: Decision-making logic appears everywhere in AI: in training loops, in activation functions, in inference pipelines.

label: Loops
intuition: A loop repeats a block of code many times. For each item in a list, or while some condition is true.
analogy: Like brushing teeth — you repeat the same stroke many times. A loop tells the computer to repeat an action.
why_it_matters: Training a neural network is literally a loop — run the data through, update the weights, repeat thousands of times.

label: Functions
intuition: A function is a named, reusable block of code. Give it inputs, get outputs back.
analogy: Like a toaster. Put bread in (input), press the lever (call the function), get toast out (output). Same every time.
why_it_matters: All AI components are functions. The forward pass of a neural network is literally a function.

label: Lists & Arrays
intuition: A list is an ordered collection of values stored together under one name.
analogy: Like a row of labelled lockers. Each locker has a number (index), and you open any one directly.
why_it_matters: Datasets in ML are lists of examples. Images are lists of pixels. Text is a list of tokens.

label: Dictionaries
intuition: A dictionary stores key-value pairs — look up any key instantly to get its value.
analogy: Like a real dictionary. Look up apple, get a red fruit. Fast, no searching required.
why_it_matters: Model configs, hyperparameters, and tokenizer vocabularies are all stored as dictionaries.

label: What is an Algorithm?
intuition: An algorithm is a step-by-step procedure that solves a problem reliably every time.
analogy: Like a GPS route. Given start and end, it gives you a precise set of turns that always gets you there.
why_it_matters: Machine learning IS an algorithm — one that learns the steps itself from data rather than being told them explicitly.

label: Big O: How Fast is Code?
intuition: Big O describes how the time an algorithm takes grows as the input gets bigger.
analogy: If you search for a name in a 10-page phone book by reading every page: 10x pages = 10x time. That's O(n). A binary search: 10x pages = 3-4x time. That's O(log n).
why_it_matters: Training efficiency, inference speed, and model architecture decisions all depend on understanding computational complexity.

label: NumPy & Arrays
intuition: NumPy gives Python the ability to do math on entire arrays at once — fast, using optimised C code underneath.
analogy: Instead of adding numbers one by one, NumPy lets you pour two buckets of numbers together and get a new bucket with all the sums instantly.
why_it_matters: NumPy is the foundation all ML libraries are built on. PyTorch tensors are essentially NumPy arrays with GPU support.

label: Vectors & Dot Products
intuition: A vector is a list of numbers representing a point in space. The dot product measures how similar two vectors are.
analogy: Imagine two arrows pointing in space. The dot product tells you how much they're pointing in the same direction.
why_it_matters: Every word, image, and concept in AI is represented as a vector. Similarity search, attention, embeddings — all dot products.

label: Matrices & Multiplication
intuition: A matrix is a 2D grid of numbers. Matrix multiplication is the key operation inside every neural network layer.
analogy: Like a grid of mixing knobs. Each knob says how much of one signal gets mixed into another. Matrix multiplication does all the mixing at once.
why_it_matters: A neural network forward pass is a sequence of matrix multiplications. Understanding this lets you read architecture diagrams.

label: Probability Basics
intuition: Probability is a number between 0 and 1 that says how likely something is. 0 = impossible. 1 = certain.
analogy: Like weather forecasting. 70% chance of rain means: in 100 similar days, it rained on about 70 of them.
why_it_matters: AI models output probabilities. A language model doesn't say the next word is cat — it says there's a 72% chance the next word is cat.

label: Problem-Solving Mindset
intuition: Good engineers break big problems into smaller ones, solve each piece, then combine. This is decomposition.
analogy: Building a LEGO castle. You don't build the whole thing at once — you do the walls, then towers, then the gate. Each piece is manageable.
why_it_matters: AI systems are complex. The engineers who build them are really good at breaking impossible-seeming problems into solvable chunks.

label: What is AI?
intuition: AI is software that can do tasks that used to require human intelligence.
analogy: Teaching a dog tricks vs programming a robot — AI learns from examples instead of following rigid rules.
why_it_matters: You're building AI — understanding what it actually is (and isn't) prevents the hype from clouding your engineering.

label: AI vs ML vs Deep Learning
intuition: AI is the broad field. ML is a subset where systems learn from data. Deep Learning is a subset of ML using neural networks.
analogy: AI = transport. ML = cars. Deep Learning = electric cars. Each is inside the one before.
why_it_matters: People mix these up constantly. Being precise makes you a better communicator and thinker.

label: Data → Model → Prediction
intuition: ML follows a fixed pattern: collect data → train a model → make predictions on new data.
analogy: Like teaching a child to recognise dogs by showing 1000 photos — then testing them on a new photo they've never seen.
why_it_matters: This three-step pipeline is the mental model behind every ML system you'll ever build.

label: Supervised Learning
intuition: Supervised learning trains on labelled examples — input paired with the correct answer.
analogy: A student learning with an answer key. For every practice problem, they see the right answer and adjust.
why_it_matters: Most practical ML — spam detection, image recognition, translation — is supervised learning.

label: Neural Networks
intuition: A neural network is a chain of matrix multiplications with non-linear functions in between.
analogy: Like a series of filters — each layer extracts progressively more abstract features from the input.
why_it_matters: Every modern AI system — LLMs, image models, voice assistants — is a neural network.

label: Transformers Architecture
intuition: The Transformer is the architecture behind GPT, Claude, and every major LLM. It processes all tokens in parallel using attention.
analogy: Like a room where everyone can see and talk to everyone else at once, instead of passing a message down a line.
why_it_matters: You cannot build or work with LLMs without understanding the Transformer architecture.

label: Embeddings
intuition: An embedding turns words, images, or any data into a dense vector where similar things are close together.
analogy: Like placing cities on a map — Paris and London are close (both European capitals), Paris and Tokyo are far.
why_it_matters: Embeddings are the bridge between raw text/images and the math that AI operates on.

label: RAG Systems
intuition: RAG gives an LLM the ability to look things up before answering.
analogy: Like an open-book exam vs closed-book. RAG lets the model check its notes before answering.
why_it_matters: Most production AI applications are RAG systems. It's the most practical AI architecture to learn.

label: AI APIs & Production
intuition: An API lets your application talk to an AI model hosted elsewhere.
analogy: Like ordering food delivery — you don't need a kitchen, just the menu (API docs) and a phone (HTTP request).
why_it_matters: Most AI applications call APIs. Building your first AI product means knowing how to use them correctly.

label: AI System Design
intuition: Designing AI systems means thinking about data flow, latency, cost, reliability, and how the AI component fits into a larger product.
analogy: Like designing a city — not just the buildings, but the roads, utilities, and how people flow through it.
why_it_matters: This is what senior AI engineers do. It's the skill that separates practitioners from architects.
